content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
, FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 428 J Street• Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Teresa L. Stricker 
Interim City Attorney 
100 Santa Rosa A venue, Room 8 
Santa Rosa, CA 95404 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-240 
Dear Ms. Stricker: 
January 4, 2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Santa Rosa Mayor John 
Sawyer regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 You 
request follow up advice to our advice in Stricker Advice Letter, No. A-16-161 that the Mayor 
could not take part in governmental decisions relating to the City's adoption of its Rent 
Stabilization and Other Tenant Protections Ordinance (the "Ordinance") due to his disqualifying 
financial interest in his residential rental business. 
This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the 
"Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when rendering advice. (In re Oglesby ( 1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71.) Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest 
provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common 
law conflict of interest or Section 1090. 
QUESTION 
Given that the Mayor had a conflict of interest in decisions relating to the City's adoption of 
the Ordinance, may he now take part in decisions relating to a referendum petition challenging the 
Ordinance pursuant to Elections Code Section 9241? 
CONCLUSION 
No. The Mayor may not take part in decisions relating to the referendum process because 
those decisions are "inextricably interrelated" to the City Council's prior decision to adopt the 
Ordinance. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. A-16-240 
Page No. 2 
You are the interim City Attorney and the authorized representative of the Mayor. The 
Mayor's spouse owns a single family residence with an attached in-law unit, and the Mayor 
separately rents out each of these residential rental properties to different tenants. 
On August 13, 2016, you submitted a request for formal written advice, asking whether the 
Act prohibited the Mayor from taking part in decisions relating to a proposed rental control 
ordinance that would impose eviction controls on certain residential rental properties within the 
City. 
In the Stricker Advice Letter, No. A-16-161, we considered whether the Mayor was 
financially interested in decisions relating to the proposed ordinance. The proposed ordinance's 
eviction controls were not applicable to the Mayor's residential rental properties, and other options 
for the proposed ordinance under consideration were not capable of segmentation. Accordingly, we 
concluded that the Act prohibited the Mayor from taking part in decisions relating to the proposed 
ordinance because those decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on 
the Mayor's financial interest in his residential rental business. 
On August 30, 2016, the City Council adopted the Ordinance. Based on our advice in the 
Stricker Advice Letter, supra, the Mayor did not take part in decisions relating to its adoption. The 
Ordinance established restrictions on rent increases and limited the permissible grounds for 
evictions with respect to certain residential rental properties within the City. Because the Ordinance, 
as adopted, does not apply to rented single family homes, including accessory or in-law units, the 
residential rental properties rented by the Mayor's business are not subject to the Ordinance. 
A timely referendum petition has now been filed challenging the Ordinance. The Sonoma 
County Registrar of Voters is currently in the process of determining whether there is a sufficient 
number of signatures of qualified voters to certify the referendum petition. If the Registrar certifies 
that the referendum petition is sufficient, the City Council must either repeal the Ordinance or 
submit it to the voters for approval pursuant to Elections Code Section 9241. If the Ordinance is 
repealed, or submitted to the voters and not approved, that law prohibits the City Council from 
enacting the Ordinance again for a period of one year after the Ordinance's repeal or disapproval. 
ANALYSIS 
Regulation 18706 governs the segmentation of a governmental decision under the Act, and 
authorizes an agency to segment a decision in which a public official has a financial interest, to 
allow participation by the official, if specified conditions are met, including the condition that the 
decision can be broken down into separate decisions that are not "inextricably interrelated" to the 
decision in which the official has the disqualifying interest. (Regulation I 8706(a)(l ).) "[D]ecisions 
are 'inextricably interrelated' when the result of one decision will effectively determine, affirm, 
nullify, or alter the result of another decision." (Regulation 18706(b).) 
At issue is whether decisions relating to the referendum petition are inextricably interrelated 
to the City Council's prior decision to adopt the Ordinance. 


File No. A-16-240 
Page No. 3 
If the City Council decides to repeal the Ordinance or submit the Ordinance to the voters for 
approval, that decision would nullify its previous decision to adopt the Ordinance. Furthermore, if 
the Mayor were allowed to take part in the decisions relating to the referendum process, he might 
influence those decisions in a manner that would protect the existing Ordinance, thereby ensuring 
that the eviction controls at issue would remain inapplicable to the residential rental properties 
rented by his business. Accordingly, we conclude that the Act also prohibits the Mayor from taking 
part in decisions relating to the referendum process. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
MFC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
Matthew F. Christy 
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
Given that the Mayor had a conflict of interest in decisions relating to the City's adoption of 
the Ordinance, may he now take part in decisions relating to a referendum petition challenging the 
Ordinance pursuant to Elections Code Section 9241?
sections.conclusion:
No. The Mayor may not take part in decisions relating to the referendum process because 
those decisions are "inextricably interrelated" to the City Council's prior decision to adopt the 
Ordinance.

QUESTION: Given that the Mayor had a conflict of interest in decisions relating to the City's adoption of 
the Ordinance, may he now take part in decisions relating to a referendum petition challenging the 
Ordinance pursuant to Elections Code Section 9241?

CONCLUSION: No. The Mayor may not take part in decisions relating to the referendum process because 
those decisions are "inextricably interrelated" to the City Council's prior decision to adopt the 
Ordinance.
